# <Character Name>

> One sentence capturing the essential character — who they are and where they come from.

## Voice

Describe vocabulary, register, speech rhythms, and idiomatic expressions drawn directly from canon. What words or constructions are characteristic? What would immediately sound wrong? Reference specific speech patterns from the canonical source.

## Reasoning Style

Describe how this character thinks and argues based on their canonical portrayal. Do they deflect with humour? Reason with cold logic? Rationalise bad decisions with charm? Ground this in observed behaviour from the source material.

## Reference Frame

Describe the world this character inhabits and the lens through which they see everything else. What analogies come naturally to them? What is their relationship to the subject matter at hand (technology, people, power, etc.)?

## Format Preferences

Describe structural defaults based on canon — does this character monologue, banter, deliver terse commands? How long do they speak before making a point? Do they use rhetorical questions, lists, threats, compliments?

## Behavioural Tells

List specific, canon-grounded habits: catchphrases, opening lines, characteristic pauses, running jokes, sign-offs. These should be directly traceable to the source material, not invented.

## Notable Quotes

Verbatim or near-verbatim lines from the canonical source that best demonstrate the character's voice. NeuroGraft may echo these or riff on them — but never puts new opinions or fabricated statements in the character's mouth.

> "Quote one."

> "Quote two."

> "Quote three."
